content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : Ken Finney                                    Date : June 3, 1976

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION      Ed Kerry

Subject: Judges--"Jurisdiction" for Purposes of Disclosing
         Investments and Interests in Real Property

I.        A decision was apparently made at the last opinion
request meeting that municipal and superior court judges
have statewide "jurisdiction" for purposes of disclosing
investments and interests in real property pursuant to
Sections 87202-87204 of the Act. I request that this sub-
ject be scheduled for further discussion at the next
opinion request meeting.

II.       Technically, all judges might be considered state
officers as Section 1, Article 6, California Constitution,
vests the judicial power of the state in the supreme court,
courts of appeal, superior courts, municipal courts and
justice courts.

III.      Practically, however, municipal and superior court
judges should be considered local officers whose jurisdic-
tion is the county and not the state, for several reasons.
First, unlike state officers, municipal court judges are
elected locally from a specific district within the
county; superior court judges are elected from the entire
county. Secondly, municipal and superior courts exist to
serve local populations. In fact, their existence is tied
to minimum local population bases (250,000 minimum for
a superior court; 40,000 minimum for a municipal court),
provided, however, that each county will have a superior
court. They do not have statewide or even regional jurisdic-
tion. Thirdly, their decisions normally have precedential
value only within the county. Fourthly, their appellate
jurisdiction, similarly, is limited to the county. Finally,
they are paid by the county--whether they are municipal or
superior court judges, and not by the state.


- 2 -

IV.     By contrast, judges of the supreme court and courts of
appeal are paid by the state, exercise jurisdiction and
take appeals in matters of statewide concern, hand down
decisions with statewide precedential impact and are
elected, either on a statewide basis or from within their
multi-county appellate districts.

V.     Accordingly, I would urge that municipal and superior
court judges be required to disclose investments and interests
in real property only within the jurisdiction of their
respective county.


EK:mra

A decision was apparently made at the last opinion request meeting that municipal and superior court judges have statewide "jurisdiction" for purposes of disclosing investments and interests in real property pursuant to Sections 87202-87204 of the Act. I request that this sub- ject be scheduled for further discussion at the next opinion request meeting. II. Technically, all judges might be considered state officers as Section 1, Article 6, California Constitution, vests the judicial power of the state in the supreme court, courts of appeal, superior courts, municipal courts and justice courts. III. Practically, however, municipal and superior court judges should be considered local officers whose jurisdic- tion is the county and not the state, for several reasons. First, unlike state officers, municipal court judges are elected locally from a specific district within the county; superior court judges are elected from the entire county. Secondly, municipal and superior courts exist to serve local populations. In fact, their existence is tied to minimum local population bases (250,000 minimum for a superior court; 40,000 minimum for a municipal court), provided, however, that each county will have a superior court. They do not have statewide or even regional jurisdic- tion. Thirdly, their decisions normally have precedential value only within the county. Fourthly, their appellate jurisdiction, similarly, is limited to the county. Finally, they are paid by the county--whether they are municipal or superior court judges, and not by the state. - 2 - IV. By contrast, judges of the supreme court and courts of appeal are paid by the state, exercise jurisdiction and take appeals in matters of statewide concern, hand down decisions with statewide precedential impact and are elected, either on a statewide basis or from within their multi-county appellate districts. V. Accordingly, I would urge that municipal and superior court judges be required to disclose investments and interests in real property only within the jurisdiction of their respective county.
